title: Cultivation of Cacti
paragraph-1: Nurturing plants involves providing the proper place and conditions for them. [cite: 277] Cacti grow in a variety of habitats, and ideally, where they are grown should mimic the conditions of their habitat. [cite: 278] As a practical matter, cacti are tolerant of a wide range of conditions but some cacti may require protection from extremes. [cite: 279] For example, many cacti are tolerant of prolonged drought, and most do best if their roots are not constantly wet. [cite: 280] Also, many cacti are adapted for absorbing water and storing it in their succulent stems, swelling as the stem accumulates water. [cite: 281] Cacti that are swollen with water may be susceptible to bursting if they are exposed to below-freezing temperatures for a sustained period. [cite: 282] When grown outdoors, cacti should be placed in soil with excellent drainage. [cite: 283] Cold-hardy plants should be selected for areas that are not frost-free. [cite: 284] Many cacti will require protection in a greenhouse, depending on the climate and weather conditions of the area in which they are cultivated. [cite: 285]
paragraph-2: Regardless of where they are grown, however, successful cultivation also involves providing cacti with the right amounts of nutrition, light, and water. [cite: 286] Close observation of the plants will prompt you to make the necessary adjustments in this triangle of plant care. [cite: 287] For example, dormant periods will occur during late fall to early spring when light levels and temperatures are lower. [cite: 288] Short periods of rest will also occur during extremely hot periods in midsummer. [cite: 289] The frequency of watering should be diminished during long periods of dormancy, and fertilizer should be withheld during both long and short periods of dormancy. [cite: 290] Growth should not be stimulated when a cactus chooses to rest.. [cite: 291]

title: Light
paragraph-1: Plants that dwell in arid lands survive through a process of waste not, want not. [cite: 293] The keys to survival for cacti are their succulence and their water-thrifty crassulacean acid metabolism (CAM), described in Chapter 1, under Physiology. [cite: 294] Plants that do not have CAM throw away as much as 90% of the water captured by their roots. [cite: 295] No recipe for water-thrifty cactus care is complete unless it is designed to take advantage of CAM. [cite: 296] [cite: 297]
paragraph-2: Plants combine the energy from light with the gases from air and the water and dissolved substances from soil to manufacture sugars and proteins. [cite: 298] Cacti use CAM to capture energy from light during the day, using the carbon dioxide captured from air during the night. [cite: 299] Sunlight is a necessity, but sunburn can be a risk, even for cacti. [cite: 300] Cultivated plants may be exposed to higher light levels than they would experience in the wild, especially in greenhouses. [cite: 301] Shade cloth can be used to prevent sunburn. It is rated according to how much light it blocks out: 60% shade cloth blocks 60% of the sun's rays. [cite: 302] At least 35% shade cloth is usually necessary for greenhouse conditions. [cite: 303] I use a cloth manufactured with narrow strips of aluminum ribbon that reflect part of the light back out of the greenhouse. [cite: 304] Other fibers, woven in to hold the strips in place, block the light. [cite: 305] This type of cloth not only produces shade but also reduces heat buildup through its reflective properties. [cite: 306]

title: Containers
paragraph-1: Cacti are commonly grown in containers, even when grown outdoors. [cite: 308] Container-grown plants are not only at the mercy of their caretaker, but also the container into which they spread their roots. [cite: 309] Twenty years of trial and error has led me to the conclusion that either plastic pots or clay pots fired at very high temperatures work best. [cite: 310] Plastic pots should be opaque, not white or semitransparent. Light that passes through a pot wall will damage roots. [cite: 311] Algae may be found choking roots near the inner surface of a white plastic pot. [cite: 312] Some brands of white plastic are also very sensitive to ultraviolet light, giving them only a short life. [cite: 313] They soon become so brittle that they fall into pieces, spilling plants and soil. [cite: 314]
paragraph-2: Clay pots should be fired at temperatures of $2000^{\circ}F$ $(1100^{\circ}C)$. This turns clay into a hard, waterproof substance. [cite: 315] If porous clays are used, the inside surface may be coated [cite: 316] with polyurethane for waterproofing. Kiln-fired glazing will also serve the same purpose. [cite: 320] Cacti that spend their summer outdoors, where they are may be subjected to excess moisture, will tolerate porous clay pots quite well. [cite: 321] Excess moisture from unexpected rains evaporates more rapidly, and rainwater will leach out accumulated fertilizer salts and hard-water minerals. [cite: 322] Wintering these same plants indoors in porous clay will, however, put them at risk. [cite: 323] Fertilizer and hard-water salts will reach toxic levels near inner pot walls. [cite: 324] Salts are left behind as the water carrying them migrates into porous walls and evaporates. [cite: 325] Roots follow water until they reach container walls. Plants may not die but they will not live as well as they would, or should, in better containers. [cite: 326]
paragraph-3: More recently I have used a thermoformed pot with two layers. [cite: 327] Coextrusion of polystyrene forms a container with a black inner layer for protection of roots from light, and an attractively colored outer layer. [cite: 328] The pots are environmentally friendly because the heavier inner black layer uses recycled, postconsumer plastics. [cite: 329] The thinner outer layer is new plastic. The use of recycled material allows these pots to be sold for about 10% less than typical solid-color ones. [cite: 330] Black has long been considered the best possible color for maximum root protection and development. [cite: 331] Now one can have the best of both, color on the outside to please, black on the inside to insure healthy roots. [cite: 332]
paragraph-4: As a general rule, deep pots are safer for plants than shallow ones. [cite: 333] Gravity causes water to move through a deep pot with greater speed. [cite: 334] A deep pot can drain more efficiently, thus creating a healthier environment for plant roots. [cite: 335] An easy way to add extra drainage holes to plastic pots is to use an electric drill to add more holes to the bottom. [cite: 336] I usually add at least four to six new holes to plastic pots. The newer thermoformed pots are an exception. [cite: 337] They provide excellent drainage and I use them just as they are. [cite: 338] Extra holes can be added to clay or ironstone pots with a hammer and a star drill bit. [cite: 339] It takes some practice to learn how hard to tap the bit with the hammer. [cite: 340] I have found that it helps to turn the pot upside down on a soft grassy surface to provide a cushion for the hammer blows. [cite: 341]
paragraph-5: A myth recycled from generation to generation is that breaking old clay pots and using the pieces at the bottom of new pots will improve drainage. [cite: 342] This practice is harmful and rather than improving drainage, restricts it. [cite: 343] All that is needed is a small piece of paper towel or tissue to prevent new soil mix from spilling out of the drainage holes. [cite: 344] A latticework of new roots will grow to hold the soil in place long before the paper disintegrates. [cite: 345]

title: Potting Media
paragraph-1: Soil is perhaps the most misunderstood basic need of cacti. [cite: 347] An animal can get up and walk away from an unfavorable environment, but plants are stuck where they are put. [cite: 348] Think of soil as a blanket that separates rock from sky. [cite: 349] Most soils are made up of 95% pulverized rock and 5% organic material. [cite: 350] It teems with bacteria, fungi, and protozoa, not to mention larger organisms. [cite: 351] Spaces between soil particles make up about half of the volume of most soils. [cite: 352] This is the air- and water-holding space. Water flows through soil following the pull of gravity, but each soil particle will be covered with a coating that is several layers thick. [cite: 353] An elaborate storehouse is formed that roots can tap. Eventually, gravity and evaporation will drain the water away, but only to remove what is left. [cite: 354] Plants must have air as well as water. Roots get their oxygen from air trapped in the same soil spaces that hold water. [cite: 355] The smaller the particles, the smaller the spaces will be for air and water. [cite: 356] And, of course, cramped spaces are more likely to be filled with water to the exclusion of air. [cite: 357] Such a soil would save a plant from dying of thirst, but will kill it by suffocation. [cite: 358]
paragraph-2: The 5% organic component of soil is made up of dead matter that is being disassembled by microorganisms. [cite: 359] Organic matter transforms soil texture from hard and harsh to soft and loose. [cite: 360] As microorganisms break organic matter down, mucilage is secreted that cements smaller soil particles together into generous crumbs. [cite: 361] This creates that soft, silky feel when well-cared-for garden loam is stroked between thumb and forefinger. [cite: 362] A gardener finds the heady aroma and touch of fine soil a sensual experience. [cite: 363] It is a great temptation to add this treasured substance to potting mix. Do not. [cite: 364] Soil is far different from the potting media we should be using. [cite: 365] Wild soil contains undesirable weed seeds, insects, and nematodes. Worst of all, some of the microorganisms in the soil can kill plants. [cite: 366] They are the hunters that go forth and kill to provide food for other microorganisms; they are the pathogens. [cite: 367] The highly organic soils that have been so successful for gardeners work in gardens, fields, and flower beds because soil microorganisms disassemble the organic content into the elements from which they were first composed. [cite: 368] Only then can the plant use them. It is important to remember that plants do not eat, they drink. [cite: 369] They cannot absorb organic material unless it is first decomposed into substances that are water soluble. [cite: 370] Then, plants manufacture their own organic molecules by the process of photosynthesis, using gases taken in through the stomata, on the stems of cacti rather than on the leaves as in most plants, and the mineral-laden water taken up through the roots. [cite: 371]
paragraph-3: Container-grown plants are kept in an artificial environment; therefore, they need an artificial soil. [cite: 373] Potting subjects plants to stresses unknown in the wild. The potting medium should store water, oxygen, and dissolved minerals. [cite: 374] Its only other function is support, to keep the plant from tipping over. [cite: 375] Water quality, frequency of watering, temperature, humidity, and pot type influence the type of potting medium needed. [cite: 376] No one medium is ideal for everyone. When you find a formula that works for you, put all your plants in it. [cite: 377] New plants will do best if repotted into your mix shortly after purchase. [cite: 378]
paragraph-4: I avoid sand in my potting media because it is so heavy that it migrates to the bottom of the pot and restricts healthy drainage. [cite: 379] Absence of oxygen allows killer bacteria to multiply explosively. They are anaerobes that grow best without oxygen. [cite: 380] These germs gain entrance through the dying roots and travel up through the stem, causing the characteristic sour odor of a cactus carcass that has succumbed to anaerobic rot. [cite: 381] It is easy to add solids to a mix, but how to add air? [cite: 382] Use light chunky substances such as perlite or pumice. This material does not pack and has numerous tiny cavities that trap and hold air. [cite: 383] At least half of the mix should be an air-trapping substance. [cite: 384] Perlite should be of horticultural grade and particle size. Wear a mask when incorporating it into the mix. [cite: 385] I prefer coarse pumice to perlite, but it may be difficult to buy in some areas. [cite: 386]
paragraph-5: I also use a special form of processed clay that has been kiln fired to create permanent little chunks with all the advantages of clay but without its disadvantages. [cite: 387] Clay is the finest reservoir for water storage because its microscopic particles attract one another in an organized way to form a lattice into which water molecules fit nicely. [cite: 388] Water is held in place until it is needed. Gravity and evaporation cannot steal it before the plant can use it. [cite: 389] Baked clay will not physically break down and restrict drainage. [cite: 390] Kiln-fired clay is used for hydroponics and can be purchased under a trade names. [cite: 391] Clay cat litter will not work; it has not been baked at temperatures high enough and will break down. [cite: 392]
paragraph-6: In addition to an air-trapping substance and a water reservoir, the potting medium should contain a high-quality commercial soilless mix. [cite: 393] These mixes contain substances such as Canadian peat moss, pine bark, coir, and composted peanut hulls; [cite: 394] some even contain rock wool. This organic material is not added as plant food but to improve air- and water-holding ability, reduce compaction, and improve the structure. [cite: 395] Also present in mixes are minerals such as perlite and vermiculite that are mined and then heated to 1400°F (760°C). [cite: 396] The high temperature makes these substances pop like pop- [cite: 397] corn, creating cavities to store air and water. Good commercial mixes are completed by adding a starter charge of fertilizer and trace elements that will last about 1 month. [cite: 398] Some brands also include wetting agents to increase water-holding capacity. [cite: 399] The best mixes drain like a sieve, hold air like a balloon, and water like a sponge. [cite: 400]
paragraph-7: My potting medium consists of the following: two parts commercial soil mix, one part hydroponic clay, and one part coarse pumice. [cite: 401] All the plants are placed in the same mix, and new plants are repotted as soon as they arrive. [cite: 402] In addition, however, I add something to the mix as a treat for my plants, the lowly earthworm supplying me with my plant dessert. [cite: 403] Dirt that goes into an earthworm's mouth emerges enriched with five times more nitrogen, seven times more phosphorus, and eleven times more potassium. [cite: 404] These are the elements plants need in the greatest abundance, and the major ingredients in fertilizers. [cite: 405] Earthworm castings can be purchased at most well-stocked garden centers. [cite: 406] They may either be mixed into the potting medium or used as a top-dressing. [cite: 407] Soil within a pot should be uniform. The practice of putting several layers of different types of mix in the same container may kill the plant. [cite: 408] Roots will be subjected to some areas of mix that are too wet, others that are too dry. [cite: 409]

title: REPOTTING
paragraph-1: Cacti should be repotted when the soil medium is fairly dry. [cite: 411] Repotting always creates some damage to root systems, and moist soil will increase the likelihood of a plant infection starting in the damaged area. [cite: 412] Wait a few days to give broken roots time to heal before watering the repotted plant. [cite: 413] Repotting rejuvenates plants by freeing the root system from potting medium that has accumulated a full complement of unused fertilizer salts and dead roots. [cite: 414]
paragraph-2: Clean the potting bench after each day of repotting. I usually cover my potting bench with a layer of newspapers, and cleanup simply involves folding the papers and depositing them in the trash. [cite: 415] Never reuse pots without sanitizing them first in a solution of bleach. [cite: 416] I use a solution of bleach to water, 1 to 30, in a plastic garbage can. [cite: 417] Pots should be soaked overnight. Transfer them to a solution of vinegar and water to remove hard-water crusting, and give them a final, fresh-water rinse. [cite: 418] Old potting mix should be discarded. Use fresh mix when repotting and store it in a closed container to keep it pest-free. [cite: 419] Keep tools clean and dry. If they have been used on a sick plant, they should be sanitized with bleach, Lysol, or Physan 20, or boiled in water. [cite: 420]
paragraph-3: If a virus is suspected, use a so- [cite: 421] lution of Physan 20, one tablespoon to a gallon of water, and soak the tool for at least 15 minutes. [cite: 424]

title: TOPDRESSING
paragraph-1: There are many reasons to use topdressing on soil surfaces around cacti. [cite: 426] Dampness around the base encourages diseases such as stem rot. [cite: 427] Gravel topdressing not only helps support the cactus, it also dries soon after watering, providing air circulation in a critical area. [cite: 428] For plants that are extremely moisture sensitive I use epoxy-coated aquarium gravel. [cite: 429] This stone is waterproof and dries almost immediately. My favorite, however, is red crushed granite, sold as coarse turkey grit. [cite: 430] It is attractive, inexpensive, and does not compact on soil surfaces. [cite: 431] Many growers use pea gravel, but it should be washed carefully before use. [cite: 432]

title: Water
paragraph-1: Water is important for plants, yet most people give little thought to water quality. [cite: 434] A pH of 7 is neutral, more than 7 alkaline, and less than 7 acidic; [cite: 435] pH is important because many minerals have trouble entering roots at the wrong pH. [cite: 436] Ideal pH is 6-7, slightly on the acid side. Other chemical properties of water can also cause problems. [cite: 437] I water my plants with well water that is very hard. [cite: 438] To compensate I repot often, at least once a year. [cite: 439] Soluble salts build up in potting media and eventually reach toxic levels. [cite: 440] Repotting gets rid of these salts and also removes dead roots that accumulate in quantity after a cactus goes through its winter resting period. [cite: 441] These dead roots are the perfect environment for plant infections. [cite: 442] Most plants start dying first in the root zone where it cannot be seen until it is too late. [cite: 443] For these reasons, I advise repotting cacti often. [cite: 444]
paragraph-2: There are three ways to water container-grown plants. Overhead watering is usually done through an attachment to a hose or a watering can called a rose. [cite: 445] The rose breaks water into soft, misty streams. A disadvantage of this method is that plants may develop disfiguring spots from minerals in the irrigation water. [cite: 446] The second method is bottom watering. Pots are usually left sitting in pans or saucers and water is added to the pan or saucer. [cite: 447] Bottom watering works well but has a couple of disadvantages: each container has to be removed from its pan or saucer at least monthly for leaching, and it is important that any excess water not taken up by the potting medium be removed before too many hours pass. [cite: 448] The third method is to water each plant at the soil line. [cite: 449] This is the method I use and find that its only major disadvantage is that it takes longer to water. [cite: 450] I turn this into an advantage by enjoying each plant as I give it a drink and looking carefully for any sign of health problems that will require action. [cite: 451]
paragraph-3: A good-quality watering can is all that is needed for smaller collections, but as plant numbers increase, some type of system will be necessary to improve watering efficiency. [cite: 452] I use a 35-gallon (130-liter) plastic garbage can, a submersible sump pump, and a 30-ft (10-m) garden hose. [cite: 453] Attached to the end of this hose is a metal watering wand with a trigger to meter water flow. [cite: 454] Each plant is watered at the soil line. [cite: 455]
paragraph-4: How often should one water? Simply plunge your finger into the soil mix. [cite: 456] If it comes out with soil particles sticking to it, the plant does not need water. [cite: 457] If no particles are clinging to the skin, the soil is dry and water is needed. [cite: 458] I generally water every 7-10 days during the growing season, and about every 4-6 weeks during dormancy. [cite: 459] More cacti are lost as the result of untimely application of water than for any other reason. [cite: 460] Even though cacti rest during their winter dormancy, the roots continue to grow all winter. [cite: 461] Growing roots need a little water throughout the winter or they will die. [cite: 462] Lack of water will not kill cacti during their dormant period, but certainly there will be root loss and stunting that may not be necessary. [cite: 463]

title: Fertilizer
paragraph-1: I add fertilizer to my irrigation solution every three of four waterings, at one-fourth to one-sixteenth of the strength recommended on the label. [cite: 465] Nutrition is supplied only during the growing season. It is important that no growth stimulation be supplied when plants are resting, during winter or summer. [cite: 466] After tiring of supplying nutrition for my cacti by chipping away at the rocks my granular fertilizers had become since the last growing season, no matter how carefully I sealed the containers, I use a liquid fertilizer. [cite: 467]
paragraph-2: It is best to buy a premium product that is supported by research. [cite: 468] Complete nutrition for container-grown plants requires more than 10 elements necessary for proper growth. [cite: 469] Good fertilizers are made from calcium nitrate, potassium nitrate, monobasic ammonium phosphate, magnesium nitrate, and if possible, a mix of soluble trace elements. [cite: 470] The nitrogen present in fertilizer is usually a blend of nitrate $(NO_{3})$ and ammonium $(NH_{4})$. [cite: 471] Too much of the wrong type of nitrogen may cause soft growth that makes plants less resistant to disease. [cite: 472] It is helpful if the label states that the formula is designed for soilless mixes. [cite: 473] Numbers on a plant food label are an international code, showing proportions of three major elements needed to support growth. [cite: 474] The first number represents nitrogen, responsible for building tissues such as stems and leaves. [cite: 475] The second number represents phosphorus, which orchestrates the growth of healthy root systems, flowering, and seed production. [cite: 476] Superphosphate is the usual source of phosphorus. [cite: 477] The third represents potassium, which increases plant resistance to stress and disease. Potash is the usual source of potassium. [cite: 478] Thus a fertilizer with a 5-10-5 label contains 5 parts nitrogen, 10 parts phosphorus, and 5 parts potassium. [cite: 479]
paragraph-3: These major elements should be supplemented with minor elements called trace elements. [cite: 480] They are called minor not because they are unimportant, but because they are required in very small quantities. [cite: 481] Nevertheless, they are necessary for plant health. Trace elements include magnesium, iron, manganese, copper, zinc, boron, and molybdenum. [cite: 482] Many newer and more expensive fertilizers contain these trace elements in a soluble sulfated form that will not precipitate out and be lost. [cite: 483] Look also for a product with buffers to help manage soil pH. [cite: 484]
paragraph-4: When selecting a fertilizer, consider choosing one that includes sulfur. [cite: 485] Generally, plants rely on irrigation water for sulfur, but most water either is deficient in sulfur or has too much. [cite: 486] Slow-growing plants such as cacti can exist at a sulfur level of 10-20 parts per million; [cite: 487] 20-30 parts per million is required by most plants. Only about 20% of the water supply of the United States contains sulfur in proper concentrations for healthy plants. [cite: 488] Soilless mixes contain very little sulfur. Gypsum or superphosphate will provide a quick fix when added to a soil mix, but they are only helpful for a short time since sulfate ions are leached out in a few weeks. [cite: 489] Avoid buying elemental sulfur because it will do more damage than good. Elemental sulfur does more harm than good; [cite: 490] it rapidly lowers soil pH and damages or kills plants. [cite: 491] If you question the sulfur content in your water supply, either send a sample to a testing laboratory or ask your water district supplier. [cite: 492] Diagnosing sulfur deficiency is difficult because the symptom in cacti is similar to that of nitrogen deficiency, stunted growth. [cite: 493]
paragraph-5: Plant nutrients are soluble salts that can accumulate until a potting mix becomes harmful. [cite: 494] Soluble salt buildup is dealt with by leaching. Leaching should be performed with each watering. [cite: 495] Apply enough water so that it drains from the bottom of the pot. [cite: 496] This means applying 15-20% more water than the container can hold. [cite: 497] Surplus soluble salts are carried away in the excess water. Even with frequent leaching it is good to repot cacti. [cite: 498]

title: Air Circulation and Ventilation
paragraph-1: Air is the source of gases that cacti and other plants use to thrive and grow. [cite: 500] It is at night that cacti, with their crassulacean acid metabolism, take in carbon dioxide through the stomata in their stems, storing it for use in photosynthesis during the day. [cite: 501] Yet it is at night that a greenhouse may be shut tight, and air circulation or ventilation may be needed. [cite: 502] Air circulation is simply the movement of air within a [cite: 503] plant growing area. Any sort of fan will circulate air. Ventilation introduces new air and exhausts old air. [cite: 505] Greenhouses also use ventilation as a method of cooling. Ventilation fans should exhaust enough air to provide one volume of air exchange per minute to a height of 8 ft (2.4 m). [cite: 506] For example, a greenhouse $20\times50$ ft $(6\times15~m)$ in area would require fans with a combined rated capacity of $20\times50\times8=8000$ cubic feet $(6\times15\times2.4\approx220$ cubic meters) per minute. [cite: 507]
paragraph-2: Greenhouses benefit from horizontal airflow fans, which are designed to move large volumes of air rapidly. [cite: 508] Horizontal airflow fans are expensive, however, and generally are recommended only for greenhouses larger than 600 square feet (56 square meters). [cite: 509] Typical household fans will be adequate in smaller greenhouses. Air circulation fans are generally left on 24 hours a day. [cite: 510] Smaller greenhouses will save energy by using two-speed fans, and larger ones will need multifan installations. [cite: 511] During winter months only one-fourth of normal air-exchange volume is required. [cite: 512] Locate the fan on one wall and air-intake louvers on the opposite wall. [cite: 513] Air-intake louvers should be at least 1.25 times larger than the fan area. [cite: 514] The bottoms of the fans and louvers should be about 3 ft (1 m) above the floor, allowing air to flow over the plants on benches. [cite: 515] Electronic thermostats, with intervals of about 1°F $(0.6^{\circ}C)$, are preferable to mechanical ones, usually with intervals of $5^{\circ}F(2.8^{\circ}C)$. [cite: 516] If a mechanical one were set at 80°F (27°C), it would run the fans until air temperature reached $75^{\circ}F(24^{\circ}C)$, wasting electricity and money. [cite: 517]

title: Cleanliness
paragraph-1: It is far more enjoyable to neglect housekeeping chores in favor of plant watching. [cite: 519] Unpleasant tasks have to be scheduled; otherwise, one does not do them. [cite: 520] Failure to keep a growing area clean will invite problems. [cite: 521] Dead tissue serves as an entryway for bacteria, fungi, and viruses. [cite: 522] A medium-sized hemostat works well for removing spine-covered dead flowers and may be purchased at pet supply stores or surgical supply houses. [cite: 523] Spilled soil and plant debris accumulating around work areas should also be removed frequently. [cite: 524] It is a bad habit to dump waste in a can and let it stay in the plant area for several weeks. [cite: 525] Insect pests use decaying organic matter as a place to lay eggs. [cite: 526]
paragraph-2: Clean growing areas will reduce dust in the air. Plants covered with dirt are not capable of using sunlight efficiently. [cite: 527] Dust can be removed efficiently with a soft-bristled brush or a spray bottle filled with distilled water and a drop or two of nonphytotoxic soap such as Ivory. [cite: 528] Water spots may be cleaned from the outside of plastic pots with a paper towel dampened a cleaner such as Armor All, a product found at auto- [cite: 529] mobile supply stores. Do not let any of it get on plant surfaces and never use it on the inside surface of a pot. [cite: 531]
paragraph-3: Keep yourself clean. If you have been doing general garden chores, wash and change clothes before tending to plants. [cite: 532] Thrips, for example, may hitchhike on your clothes. Keep only as many plants as you have time to care for; [cite: 533] enjoy each one. [cite: 534]

title: Pests
paragraph-1: Trying to eradicate every single insect from cultivated cacti is not only an impossible task that destroys the fun of growing plants, it would also indiscriminately kill benign or even beneficial insects along with those that truly are pests. [cite: 536] Also, good cultivation practices make cacti less attractive to six- and eight-legged pests. [cite: 537] Pests are more trouble to weak, unthrifty plants. Successful growers satisfy themselves with control rather than cure. [cite: 538] Pest control must become pest management. This is achieved by careful observation to identify pests and their approximate numbers. [cite: 539] Strategically placed yellow and blue sticky cards will capture the flying insects, and a good magnifying glass will help find the crawlers. [cite: 540] All new plants should be isolated before joining the rest of a collection. [cite: 541] Three weeks is usually adequate to detect pest problems. If problems are detected, then the isolated plants can be treated. [cite: 542] New plants should also be repotted before leaving isolation to rid them of potentially infected soil. [cite: 543]

title: THRIPS
paragraph-1: There are hundreds of kinds of thrips but they all damage cacti similarly. [cite: 545] Thrips feed by scraping and rasping tender stem surfaces; however, their favorite food is pollen. [cite: 546] The best way to look for thrips is to tap a flower or blow on it. [cite: 547] Thrip-infected flowers will show a flurry of activity as the fast-moving insects scurry out of pollen sacs to the edge of a petal. [cite: 548] Next they will swing over the edge to find a hiding place underneath. [cite: 549] Other signs are blotches or streaks of pollen grains on flowers, as well as deformed and undersized blossoms. [cite: 550] Cactus epidermis will appear finely speckled with many tiny yellow spots. [cite: 551] There may be a scattering of tiny black specks, thrip feces, on flowers. [cite: 552]
paragraph-2: Thrips are about 0.1 in (2 mm) long and move rapidly for their size. [cite: 553] To the naked eye they may look like a short worm with legs. [cite: 554] Both larvae and adults have a similar appearance, except adults have wings. [cite: 555] During summer thrips are found almost everywhere outdoors and they enter houses and greenhouses through doors, windows, and vents. [cite: 556] They can crawl through normal screening, and pets, people, and fresh flowers and fruits may introduce them into the plant growing area. [cite: 557] Thrips are prolific. Reproduction can occur with or [cite: 558] without mating, and each female will lay 50-200 eggs. Eggs hatch in a few days, and larvae begin feeding on plants immediately. [cite: 559] They become adults in 7-10 days and the cycle begins again. [cite: 560]
paragraph-3: The key to thrip control is early detection. Thrips prefer warm temperatures and low humidity. [cite: 561] They do the most damage when temperatures are $90^{\circ}F$ $(32^{\circ}C)$ and higher. [cite: 562] Many species of thrips live part of their life by pupating in soil, and I prefer the precision of applying a granular systemic insecticide to the soil. [cite: 563]

title: SPIDER MITES
paragraph-1: Spider mites are the most common pests of plants cultivated indoors. [cite: 565] Mite-infested plants will be freckled with little yellow marks where mites have been sucking plant juices. [cite: 566] In heavy infestations there may be fine webbing on plant tops or between spines. [cite: 567] Heavily damaged plants will probably scar with a light tan to brown callus. [cite: 568] Mites are so tiny they can float on wind currents. [cite: 569] They may also be introduced indoors on clothing or the coats of pets. [cite: 570] They are about the size of a pinhead and can best be found with a 10x lens. [cite: 571] The most common species is the two-spotted mite. It is yellowish tan to greenish in color and has two dark spots, one on each shoulder. [cite: 572] The older the mite, the larger the spots. [cite: 573]
paragraph-2: At $70^{\circ}F(21^{\circ}C)$ a generation of spider mites is completed in about 2 weeks, and above 86°F $(30^{\circ}C)$ it just takes a week. [cite: 574] Below 50°F (10°C) mites go dormant. Hibernation is also triggered as days become short in the fall. [cite: 575] They crawl off to sleep through the winter in cracks and crevices. [cite: 576] A warm, heated greenhouse will counteract this impulse in a few mites, so some damage may be seen during winter. [cite: 577]
paragraph-3: A more recently introduced chemical to manage mites is made from cinnamon oil. It controls all stages, including eggs. [cite: 578] Field trials show that it kills most mites the first day and also provides fungicidal activity. [cite: 579] Powdery mildew is controlled for many weeks after just one application. [cite: 580]

title: MEALYBUGS
paragraph-1: Mealybugs are scale insects that look like tiny wood lice and are about 0.25 in (6 mm) long. [cite: 582] They are covered with a fluffy wax that waterproofs them and makes them resistant to many water-based insecticides. [cite: 583] Mealybug nests are small masses of woolly, cottonlike substance tucked away in cracks and crevices of plants or their surroundings. [cite: 584] Nests may be found on spines, between tubercles, or among dead flowers. [cite: 585] Some mealybugs prefer living in soil among roots, and others complete their lives out in the open on plant surfaces. [cite: 586] Eggs and a few stray adults may be located in areas away from plants, leading to reinfection. [cite: 587] Male mealybugs have wings and can [cite: 588] fly, so an infestation can spread rapidly. Control rather than cure is needed. [cite: 589]
paragraph-2: I prefer treating for mealybugs with a systemic insecticide, which controls any soil dwellers as well as those found topside. [cite: 590] All insecticides are dangerous and should be used with caution. [cite: 591] If the same insecticide is used again and again, surviving mealybugs will become resistant. [cite: 592] A biological control marketed for mealybugs is Cryptolaemus montrouzieri, a black ladybug beetle from Australia. [cite: 593] Cryptolaemus worked poorly for me on cacti because the spines prevented them from reaching their prey easily. [cite: 594]

title: FUNGUS GNATS
paragraph-1: Fungus gnats are tiny, brown to black flies that cruise the airspace around plants. [cite: 596] Fungus gnats can be distinguished from whiteflies by their habits in addition to their color. [cite: 597] Fungus gnats do not spend as much time resting as whiteflies often do, but sticky yellow cards are a good way to detect an infestation. [cite: 598] On closer observation, a few fungus gnats can be seen emerging from the soil. [cite: 599] Adults do not feed on plants or damage them, but larvae do considerable damage. [cite: 600] Larval forms look like tiny worms and live in the top 2 in (5 cm) of potting media. [cite: 601] They wiggle through the soil, feeding on organic matter, including plant roots. [cite: 602] Large plants with complex root networks suffer little harm from this root pruning, but for seedlings root loss can be devastating. [cite: 603] Fungus gnats can destroy a seed flat in a few weeks. [cite: 604] Those seedlings that are not killed will have stunted growth. [cite: 605] The best control is achieved with an insecticidal soil drench, which attacks the problem at the site of plant damage. [cite: 606]

title: WHITEFLIES
paragraph-1: Whiteflies look like tiny white moths resting on plant surfaces. [cite: 608] They rarely fly unless disturbed, and then they just hover in the air for a while before landing. [cite: 609] Larval forms will also be found on plant surfaces and look like glistening small white scales. [cite: 610] All stages from larvae to adults suck juice from the host plant. [cite: 611] Most of it is defecated onto plant surfaces as a sticky foul material called honeydew. [cite: 612] Black sooty mold grows rapidly on this honeydew and can also harm the plant. [cite: 613]
paragraph-2: Two species cause the most distress, the greenhouse whitefly and the sweet-potato whitefly. [cite: 614] It is easy to identify each by carefully examining the wings of adults. [cite: 615] The greenhouse whitefly's wings are arranged flat on its back parallel to the ground; [cite: 616] the sweet-potato whitefly's wings are attached to the sides of its back at a $45^{\circ}$ angle to the ground. [cite: 617] It is important to know the species because sweet-potato whitefly is harder to control and may require chemical application for a longer [cite: 618] period of time. A systemic insecticide applied to the surface of the soil is often used for control. [cite: 620]

title: PESTICIDES
paragraph-1: Caregivers of plants must on occasion use pesticides as part of a control program. [cite: 622] Pesticides are usually classified as poisons and present health risks for the three p's-people, pets, and plants if they are handled carelessly. [cite: 623] Take time to do the job properly. [cite: 624]
paragraph-2: Spraying should be accomplished with the smallest droplet size possible. If a sprayer cannot deliver a fine spray, replace it. [cite: 625] Research has shown that the coverage of a spray increases eightfold each time droplet sized is halved. [cite: 626] One drop of liquid properly spread covers 0.3 square inch (2 square centimeters); [cite: 627] the same amount of liquid delivered as a fine aerosol will cover up to 10 square inches (65 square centimeters). [cite: 628] Delivering spray as a fine mist will cover more area with less pesticide, not only reducing health risk and saving money but also doing a better job of getting the spray into cracks and crevices. [cite: 629]
paragraph-3: When spraying, skin should be covered. Wear a hat, mask, long sleeves, and full-length trousers. [cite: 630] Hands should be covered with rubber gloves, either when spraying or when applying solid pesticides. [cite: 631] Disposable gloves and masks can be purchased inexpensively at pharmacies. [cite: 632] Though you are protected, other people, pets, and plants are not. [cite: 633] Use good judgment and foul the environment as little as possible. [cite: 634]
paragraph-4: Whenever mixing sprays, use either distilled water or water purified through reverse osmosis to dilute chemicals. [cite: 635] Hard water of the wrong pH destroys many pesticides before they are applied. [cite: 636] For example, organophosphates or carbamates diluted with alkaline water are hydrolyzed, rendering them ineffective as pesticides. [cite: 637] There are products that improve the efficiency of sprays, called spray adjuvants. (1) Buffering compounds change the pH of solutions. [cite: 638] Most often water is too alkaline, so acids are used to lower the pH. [cite: 639] (2) Spreaders, sometimes called wetting agents, allow sprays to cover plant surfaces more thoroughly. [cite: 640] Some pesticide concentrates already contain a spreader, so read the label before adding more of something that may already be there. [cite: 641] (3) Spreader-sticker is even more efficient, acting not only as a wetting agent but also encapsulating the chemical and holding it against the plant surface. [cite: 642] Spreader-sticker prevents the pesticide from washing off in rain or during overhead watering and is needed when spraying outdoor plants. [cite: 643] (4) Extenders act as a sunblock, preventing ultraviolet light from destroying chemicals before they have done their job. [cite: 644] Extending the period of chemical control in this way will allow spraying less often. [cite: 645]
paragraph-5: The benefits of pesticides outweigh their dangers when they are used properly and safely. [cite: 648] Each wise plant caregiver should follow 10 commandments: [cite: 649] 1. Read the label. Be sure the chemical is effective for the problem, that it is diluted properly, and used at proper intervals. [cite: 650] 2. Agitate. Periodically shake the spray bottle or tank when spraying. [cite: 651] Most pesticides are in suspension and will settle to the bottom. That thick slurry would damage plants. [cite: 652] 3. Do not mix pesticides. Mixing several pesticides together instead of applying them separately will often damage plants. [cite: 653] 4. Time the application properly. Apply sprays early in the morning, after sundown, or on cloudy days to reduce wastage and the possibility of harming plants. [cite: 654] Do not apply chemicals if air temperature is warmer than $85^{\circ}F$ $(30^{\circ}C)$. [cite: 655] 5. Spray carefully. Avoid drenching the soil surface with foliar sprays because many are not intended for use around roots. [cite: 656] This is particularly true for seedlings. 6. Avoid moisture stress. Plants should be recently watered. [cite: 657] Water-stressed plants are more easily damaged by chemicals. [cite: 658] 7. Do not fertilize. Do not apply pesticides with fertilizer. 8. Mix small amounts and use immediately. [cite: 659] Most products cannot be stored after dilution because they break down and become ineffective. [cite: 660] 9. Do not use damaged products. Do not use a wettable powder if it fails to break up and suspend properly, and do not use an emulsifiable concentrate if it fails to form a milky suspension. [cite: 661] 10. Ventilate. Mix chemicals in a well-ventilated area. [cite: 662]

title: Propagation - SEEDS
paragraph-1: The ideal method for germinating seeds is to provide a moist but not saturated potting medium for seeds to germinate on and grow into. [cite: 665] Seeds need little water to germinate. After being moistened they must not dry out, however, as germination will be delayed or the seed may die. [cite: 666] Most seeds dry [cite: 667] out rapidly because they have a large surface area and very little volume. [cite: 668] A closed environment is acceptable during germination because fluctuations in moisture are detrimental during this critical period. [cite: 669] The first week after germination is one of the most important periods in the life of a cactus. [cite: 670] If the soil is too wet when roots emerge, root growth will be stalled and only top growth will occur. [cite: 671] Top growth must be supported by proper root growth. Covers should come off seed flats 7-10 days after germination. [cite: 672] Moisture condensing on the cover drips down on the newly emerging seedlings, inviting damping off and poor root growth. [cite: 673]
paragraph-2: In addition to moisture, temperature is a most important factor. [cite: 674] Most seeds will germinate over a fairly wide temperature range, but somewhere between 65 and $85^{\circ}T$ $(18-30^{\circ}C)$ is best. [cite: 675] After germination, soil temperatures of $70-72^{\circ}F(21-$ $22^{\circ}C$ promote the best root growth. Excessive temperatures promote top growth but stunt root growth. If temperatures are too high, the entire plant will stop growing. For this reason, seedlings less than a month old should not be taken to the greenhouse and subjected to high air temperatures. [cite: 676]
paragraph-3: With rare exceptions, do not use fertilizers until seedlings are established and at least several weeks old. [cite: 677] All the food they need during this period is stored in the seed. [cite: 678] Fungicides and insecticides should also be avoided for as many months as possible. [cite: 679] Low light levels are adequate for germination, but once seedlings are established higher light levels are necessary for optimal growth. [cite: 680] High light levels will produce a shorter, tougher plant, but there is a risk that growth will stall at higher temperatures. [cite: 681] If you have a greenhouse, do not be afraid to take seedlings into the greenhouse when they are several months old. [cite: 682] Be sure to start them out in a shaded area until they become accustomed to higher temperatures, and then slowly move them out to areas with higher light levels. [cite: 683]

title: CUTTINGS
paragraph-1: Cacti may also be propagated by taking cuttings. The cuttings should be allowed to air-dry for several weeks before being placed in potting media for rooting. [cite: 685] This allows the cut to begin healing, by forming a thin protective callus layer before the cutting is buried in moist soil. [cite: 686] Whole stems of fairly new growth make the best cuttings. [cite: 687]
